title: Possessivartikel
explainer_md:
Possessive articles say **whose** something is: *mein* (my), *dein* (your), *sein* (his/its), *ihr* (her/their), *unser* (our), *euer* (your, plural), *Ihr* (your, formal).

**First step:** pick the right stem for the owner.

| Owner | Possessive |
|---|---|
| ich | mein |
| du | dein |
| er/es | sein |
| sie (she) | ihr |
| wir | unser |
| ihr (you pl.) | euer |
| sie/Sie (they/formal) | ihr/Ihr |

**Second step:** add the same ending that *ein* would take — possessives behave exactly like *ein*.

| | m | f | n | pl |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominativ | mein | mein**e** | mein | mein**e** |
| Akkusativ | mein**en** | mein**e** | mein | mein**e** |
| Dativ | mein**em** | mein**er** | mein**em** | mein**en** |
| Genitiv | mein**es** | mein**er** | mein**es** | mein**er** |

*Das ist **mein** Bruder.* — That is my brother. (Nominativ)

*Ich besuche **meinen** Bruder.* — I visit my brother. (Akkusativ: like einen)

*Er hilft **seiner** Mutter.* — He helps his mother. (helfen + Dativ, feminine → seiner)

*Ist das **eure** [[Katze]]?* — Is that your cat? (euer drops the middle **e**: eu**e**re → eure)

**Tip:** the owner decides the stem (*sein* = his, *ihr* = her), but the **noun after it** decides the ending. *Sie sucht **ihren** [[Schlüssel]]* — her key, and der Schlüssel is masculine Akkusativ → -en.

payload.explanation: After „[[sein]]“ (to be) the noun stays in the Nominativ: mein Bruder.
„[[besuchen]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: mein → meinen (der Bruder).
The owner is male → sein-. „[[lieben]]“ takes the Akkusativ; feminine: seine (die Mutter).
The owner (Anna) is female → ihr-. „[[suchen]]“ takes the Akkusativ: ihr → ihren (der Schlüssel).
„[[helfen]]“ takes the Dativ. Masculine: unser → unserem (der Bruder).
euer drops the middle -e- before an ending: euer + e → eure (die Katze, Nominativ).
„[[mit]]“ takes the Dativ. Feminine: ihre → ihrer (die Schwester); the owner is female → ihr-.
The owner (Peter) is male → sein-. „[[mit]]“ takes the Dativ; neuter: sein → seinem (das Auto).
„[[sehen]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: mein → meinen.
After „[[sein]]“ (to be) → Nominativ. Feminine: dein → deine (die Tasche).
„[[danken]]“ takes the Dativ. Masculine: unser → unserem.
„[[ohne]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: ihr → ihren.
„[[mit]]“ takes the Dativ. Plural: euer → euren — euer drops the middle -e- before an ending.
After „[[sein]]“ (to be) → Nominativ. Feminine: meine (die Schwester).

payload.translation: I am looking for my key.
payload.explanation: „[[suchen]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: mein → meinen (der Schlüssel).

payload.translation: He is helping his father today.
payload.explanation: „[[helfen]]“ takes the Dativ: sein → seinem (der Vater). „[[heute]]“ can stand before or after the object.

payload.translation: The car belongs to her mother.
payload.explanation: „[[gehören]]“ takes the Dativ. Feminine: ihre → ihrer (die Mutter).

reasons: It is the direct object of „besuchen“
It is the subject of the sentence
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[besuchen]]“ takes a direct object → Akkusativ. Masculine: mein → meinen (der Onkel).

payload.sentence: Sie fährt mit **ihrer Schwester** in die Stadt.
reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It follows „mit“, which always takes the Dativ
It is the direct object of the verb
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[mit]]“ is a Dativ preposition. Feminine: ihre → ihrer (die Schwester).

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It is the direct object of the verb
It is the indirect object (recipient)
It follows a dative preposition
payload.explanation: Euer Haus is the subject → Nominativ, so euer keeps its base form (no ending, no elision).
die Schwester is feminine, Nominativ → deine (like eine).
„[[besuchen]]“ + Akkusativ, plural → unsere (plural Akkusativ = plural Nominativ).
„[[helfen]]“ takes the Dativ. der Bruder → masculine Dativ: ihrem.
der Ball is masculine and the subject (Nominativ) → euer without ending.
„[[suchen]]“ + Akkusativ, but das Handy is neuter → sein stays without ending (like ein).
„[[mit]]“ always takes the Dativ. der Hund → masculine Dativ: unserem.
die Tante → feminine Akkusativ: meine (no change from Nominativ).

payload.sentence: Er fährt mit **seinem Fahrrad** zur Schule.
reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It is the direct object of „fahren“
It follows „mit“, which always takes the Dativ
It shows possession
payload.explanation: „[[mit]]“ always takes the Dativ: das Fahrrad → seinem Fahrrad.
der Vater is the subject → Nominativ, so dein takes no ending (like ein).
After „[[sein]]“ (to be) → Nominativ. Neuter das Kind: unser without ending.
„[[lieben]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: mein → meinen (der Hund).
„[[besuchen]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Feminine: sein → seine (die Oma).
„[[bei]]“ always takes the Dativ. Plural Dativ ends in -n: ihre → ihren (die Eltern).
„[[ohne]]“ takes the Akkusativ, but das Auto is neuter → unser stays without ending (like ein).
„[[helfen]]“ takes the Dativ. Neuter: sein → seinem (das Kind).

payload.prompt: Herr Müller, ist das ___ Tasche? (Sie, formal)
payload.explanation: The formal possessive Ihr is always capitalized. Feminine Nominativ: Ihr → Ihre (die Tasche).
The owner is female → ihr-. „[[suchen]]“ takes the Akkusativ; feminine: ihre (die Brille).
After „[[sein]]“ (to be) → Nominativ. Neuter das Buch: dein without ending.
Feminine Nominativ takes -e: unser → unsere (die Lehrerin).
„[[ohne]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Feminine: mein → meine (die Schwester).
„[[danken]]“ takes the Dativ. Plural Dativ: sein → seinen (die Freunde).
„[[mit]]“ takes the Dativ. Feminine: euer → eurer — euer drops the middle -e- before an ending.
After „[[sein]]“ (to be) → Nominativ. Neuter das Auto: mein without ending.
„[[besuchen]]“ takes the Akkusativ. Masculine: ihr → ihren (der Onkel).
„[[helfen]]“ takes the Dativ. Feminine: unsere → unserer (die Oma).

payload.translation: He is going to Berlin with his brother.
payload.explanation: „[[mit]]“ takes the Dativ. Masculine: sein → seinem (der Bruder).

reasons: It is the subject of the sentence
It is the direct object of the verb
It follows a dative preposition
It is the indirect object (recipient)
payload.explanation: Meine Schwester is the subject of „[[wohnen]]“ → Nominativ. Feminine: meine.

reasons: It is the direct object of „gehören“
„gehören“ always takes a Dativ object
It is the subject of the sentence
It follows an accusative preposition
payload.explanation: „[[gehören]]“ is a Dativ verb. Masculine: unser → unserem (der Lehrer).
